lesson.title: Risk Management
lesson.body: The Sharpe ratio S = (E[R] − Rƒ)/σ measures risk-adjusted return. The Kelly criterion gives the optimal fraction of capital to bet: f* = edge/odds = (μ − Rƒ)/σ² for a normal distribution. Value at Risk (VaR) at confidence level α is the loss exceeded with probability 1−α. Maximum drawdown MDD = max over t of [peak − trough] / peak measures the worst loss from a peak.

rooms.question: A strategy has annualized return 15% and std dev 20%. With Rƒ = 3%, the Sharpe ratio is:
rooms.explanation: Sharpe = (15%−3%)/20% = 12%/20% = 0.6. A Sharpe of 0.6 is decent for a single strategy; 1.0+ is excellent.

rooms.question: The Kelly criterion says to bet fraction f* = μ/σ² of capital (simplified, Rƒ=0). If strategy has μ=12% and σ=20%, f* is:
answers: 300% (full Kelly, often use 25-50% of Kelly in practice)
rooms.explanation: Full Kelly = 0.12/0.04 = 3 = 300% of capital (leveraged). Full Kelly maximizes long-run growth but has extreme drawdowns. In practice, use 25-50% of Kelly (fractional Kelly).

rooms.question: The 95% VaR of a portfolio means:
answers: The portfolio will lose more than this amount only 5% of the time
The expected loss in the worst 5% of cases
rooms.explanation: VaR_{95%} is the loss quantile at 5% probability: we expect to exceed this loss on 5% of days. It says nothing about the severity of losses beyond this threshold.

rooms.question: Order these risk metrics from most to least common in use:
rooms.explanation: Sharpe is universal. Max drawdown is widely watched by investors. VaR is required by regulators. Calmar is used by CTAs but less universal.

rooms.question: CVaR (Conditional Value at Risk) improves on VaR by:
answers: Measuring the expected loss given that losses exceed VaR
rooms.explanation: CVaR = Expected Shortfall = E[loss | loss > VaR]. It quantifies the average severity of tail losses, not just the threshold — capturing tail risk that VaR ignores.

boss.name: The Black Swan
boss.taunt: Your VaR model said I was impossible. I happened anyway.

questions.question: The Calmar ratio is defined as:
questions.explanation: Calmar ratio rewards strategies with good returns relative to their worst historical loss. A Calmar > 1 is considered good.

questions.question: Using 'full Kelly' position sizing leads to:
answers: Maximum long-run geometric growth but extreme drawdowns (often 50%+)
questions.explanation: Full Kelly maximizes log-wealth growth but allows very large drawdowns. In practice, fractional Kelly (50% or 25% of Kelly) is preferred for drawdown control.

questions.question: Normal VaR underestimates risk because financial returns have:
answers: Fat tails (more extreme events than normal distribution predicts)
questions.explanation: Financial returns exhibit fat tails (excess kurtosis). 2008-style crashes are much more likely than Gaussian VaR predicts. Use t-distribution or historical simulation for better estimates.
